text: The surgeon begins the operation with a teardrop-shaped incision extending cranially and caudally along the midline. The perineal sinus is dissected down to its base using a combination of sharp dissection and electrocautery. Assistance is provided to reach the base of the deep cavity. After mobilizing the sinus, the surgeon ensures complete clearance of affected tissue, including excision of the base of the coccyx, before flap placement.

The surgeon begins constructing the eye gap flap by defining a skin paddle centered around the inferior gluteal artery perforation. The incision is made along a line between the posterior superior iliac spine and the ischial tuberosity, at the level of the piriformis. The flap is created by incising through the subcutaneous and fascial layers down to the gluteus maximus muscle, ensuring no damage to underlying structures.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the fasciocutaneous artery perforator (FAP) flap in a lateral to medial direction within the sub-fascial layer, carefully dissecting to avoid damage to the perforating vessels consistently found in this area.

The surgeon identifies perforating vessels and mobilizes them from the gluteus maximus muscle by dissecting along the line of fiber travel. The dissection allows for good rotation of the resulting flap, demonstrating the degree of mobility achieved.

The surgeon advances the flap into the perineal defect, identifying and de-epithelializing the buried portion. Multiple perforating vessels, including an artery and a vein, are visible passing through the gluteus maximus muscle into the eye gap flap. The flap is secured superiorly to the superficial part of the sacrum and inferiorly to the remaining pelvic inlet, ensuring well-vascularized tissue at the base of the wound.
The surgeon places a suction drain underneath the flap, extending to the base of the perineal defect. The wound is then closed in layers using a standard VY closure technique.
